text: EGGE, a river of Germany, which rises near Aalen in Wurtemberg, and flows into the Danube near Dillingen.—Also a mountain-range in Prussian Westphalia, connecting the Westerwald with the Lippeschenwalde. It has an alt. of 1,820 ft.

EGGEGRUND, a village of Norway, in the prov. of Norrland, without the S entrance to Gifle bay, in N lat. 60° 43′ 30″, long. 35° 42′ 30″ E of Ferroe. A stationary light, 53 ft. above sea-level, is exhibited
EGGENBERG. See EGENBURG.
EGGENFELDEN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Bavaria, on the Rott, 12 m. SSE of Dingelfingen. Pop. 1,120.
EGGENSTEIN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, 4 m. N of Carlsruhe. Pop.
EGGERON, a village of Upper Egypt, 7 m. S of Atfieh.
EGGERSRIED, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Saint-Gall. Pop. 1,479.
EGGESFORD, a parish in Devon, 3 m. S of Chumbergh. Area 2,500 acres. Pop. 168.

EGG HARBOUR (GREAT), an inlet on the coast of New Jersey, U. S., 5 m. long, and from 1 to 4 m. broad, which receives a river of the same name, having a course of 45 m. The township contains the vs. of Bargaintown and Somers' Point. Pop.
EGG HARBOUR (LITTLE), a bay on the same coast, about 17 m. NE of Great Egg harbour. It receives Mulicus river, which is navigable 25 m. for vessels of 60 tons.—The township has a pop. of 1,875.
EGGINTON, a parish in Derbyshire, 4 m. NNE of Burton-on-Trent. Area 2,030 acres. Pop. 374. -Also a chapelry in the p. of Leighton-Buzzard, in Bedfordshire. Pop. 390.
EGG ISLAND, a small island in the straits of Magalhaen, 7 m. NE of York-Minster.—Also a small island on the NE side of Delaware bay, in Cumberland co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S.
EGGIWYL, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, bail. of Signau, on the Emme. Pop. 2,448.
EGGLESTONE, a chapelry in the p. of Middleton, in Durhamshire, 6 m. NNW of Barnardcastle. Area 7,700 acres. Pop. 617.
EGGLETON, a township in the p. of Bishop's Frome, Herefordshire, 9 m. ENE of Hereford. Pop.
EGGMUHL. See ECKMUHL.
EGGOLSHEIM, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, 2 m. N of Forchheim. Pop. 918.

EGHAM, a parish and village in Surrey, 2 m. W of Staines, and 18 m. W of London. Area 7,440 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,190; in 1831, 4,203; in 1841, CARACAMARA MARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA CARACA C EGL 4,448. The v. is delightfully situated on the banks of the Thames, which here separates Surrey from Middlesex; and is connected with the town of Staines by an iron-bridge. The great road from London to the W passes through both villages. Before the Great Western and Southampton railroads were opened, between 70 and 80 long stages passed through E. during the day: there are none now, and several of the inns are entirely closed. An annual fair is held here on 19th Sept., when races are annually held in Runymede, the plains of which are celebrated as the place appointed by King John for holding the famous conference with his barons which terminated in his signing Magna Charta.
EGIDIEN (Sr.), a village of Saxony, in the co. of Schönburg, SE of Glaucha. Pop. 1,104.
EGINA. See ÆGINA.
EGITSBORG (VESTER), a small town of Denmark, in Sieland, 8 m. SSE of Nestvid.
EGLETON, a parish in the co. of Rutland, 2 m. SE of Oakham, on a branch of the Gwash. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. in 1801, 135; in 1841, 138.
EGLETONS, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of the Corrèze. The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1841, 6,631.—The com., 15 m. NE of Tulle, had a pop. of 1,253.
EGLINGEN, a small town of Wurtemberg, 6 m. S of Nordlingen, in the Jaxt circle. Pop. 614.

EGLINGHAM, an extensive parish and township in Coquetdale ward, Northumberland, 6 m. NW of Alnwick, comprising the townships of Bassington, Beanley, New Bewick, Old Bewick, Brandon, Branton, Crawley, E., Hareup, or Harehope, Hedgeley, E Lilburn, W Lilburn, Titlington, Wooperton. The Breamish flows through the p. Area 17,650 acres. Pop. of p. in 1801, 1,341; in 1841, 1,648. Limestone and coal abound in this district.
EGLISAU, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Zurich, pleasantly situated on the r. bank of the Rhine, 10 m. SW of Schaffhausen, at an alt. of 1,043 ft. above sea-level.
EGLISE (RIVIERE, DE L'), a small river of Lower Canada, which falls into the St. Lawrence from the N, 44 m. below the island of Orleans.
EGLISE-NEUVE-D'ENTRARGUES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Besse. Pop. 2,070.
EGLISE-NEUVE-PRE'S-BI'LLOM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pny-de-Dome, cant. of Billom. Pop. 1,606.
EGLISH, a parish in King's co., 4 m. NNE of Birs. Area 14,799 acres. Pop. in 1841, 3,494.— Also a p. in co. Armagh, 4 m. N by E of Tynan. Pop. 5,601.—Also a v. in the p. of Drumglass, co. Tyrone.
EGLISHAY. See Eagleshay.
EGLISOLLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Viverols. Pop. 1,133.
EGLOFS, a village of Wurtemberg, in the Danube circle, SE of Wangen. Pop. 150.
EGLOFSHEIM, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Pfalz, SE of Regensburg. Pop. 476.
EGLOFSTEIN, a village of Franconia, in the circle of the Maine, to the E of Ebermannstadt.
EGLOSHAYLE, a parish in Cornwall, 3 m. SE of Wade-bridge, on the E bank of the river Camel, across which there is here a fine old bridge. Area 6,170 acres. Pop. in 1801, 780; in 1841, 1,357.
EGLOSKERRY, a parish in Cornwall, 3 m. WNW of Launceston. Area 3,060 acres. Pop. in 1801, 307; in 1841, 552.
EGLWYS-BREWIS, a parish in the co. of Glamorgan, 4 m. SE of Cowbridge. Pop. in 1801, 33; in 1841, 24.
EGLWYS-CYMMYN, a parish in the co. of Car- ----
EGLWYS-FAIR-ACHYRIG, a chapelry in the p. of Henllan-Amgoed, co. of Carmarthen, 6 m. ENE of Narberth, on the Taff. Pop. in 1801, 222; in 1841. 288.

EGLWYS-ILAN, a parish in Glamorganshire, 91 m. NW of Cardiff. Pop. in 1801, 865; in 1841, 3.813. There are large coal mines in this vicinity. The Pont-y-Prydd, or New bridge, stretches over the river Taff in this p., by a single arch 140 ft. in the chord, and 35 ft. in height above the level of the river at low water.
EGLWYS-RHOS, a parish in the co. of Carnarvon, 24 m. NNE of Conwy, near the mouth of the river Conwy. Pop. in 1801, 269; in 1841, 630.
EGMANTON, a parish in the co. of Nottingham, 11 m. S of Tuxford. Area 2,220 acres. Pop. in 1801, 267; in 1841, 391.
EGMEDIO, a small island in the Romanzoff group, in N lat. 9° 26' 46", W long. 189° 43'. It is 3,276. about 1 m. in length, and 1d of a mile in breadth.
EGMERE, a parish in the co. of Norfolk, 23 m. W by N of Walsingham. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. in 1801, 32; in 1841, 54.

EGMONT, the name of three villages in Holland, rentes. in the prov. of N. Holland, on the sea coast, a few miles W of Alkmaer. That nearest to the sea is called Egmond-aan-Zee, and was the scene of a bloody but indecisive action between the Gallo-Batavian and Anglo-Russian armies in October 1799. Its pop. is about 1,300.—Farther inland and on the other side of a ridge of sand-hills, lies Egmond-opden-Hoef, with 800 inhabitants. It was destroyed by the Spaniards in 1573, and the ruins of the old town are still conspicuous.—About 14 m. S of it stands Egmond-Binnen or Inner Egmont.

EGMONT BAY, a bay on the SW coast of Prince Edward's island, in the gulf of St. Lawrence, in N lat. 46° 34'. It is a large open bay 16 m. broad, and about 10 m. deep. There is no harbour within it for large vessels. There are three small vs. on the E side of the bay occupied by about 40 Acadian French families.

EGMONT ISLAND, a small island in the gulf of Mexico, on the W coast of East Florida, at the entrance of Spiritu Santo bay, in N lat. 27° 54'.- Also an island in the S. Pacific, in S lat. 19° 24', 6 m. in length, and 4 m. in breadth. It is low, and covered with trees.—See also CRUZ (SANTA).

EGMONT (Mount), a mountain at the SW extremity of the N island of New Zealand, 18 m. S of New Plymouth, in S lat. 39° 15'. Alt. 8,840 ft. It is of volcanic formation, but shows no signs of recent activity. It rises from a generally level country, by a gradual ascent, from a circle about 30 m. in diam., which forms the circumf. of its base. Half the area of this circle is perhaps sufficiently level for cultivation. Numerous streams take their rise in the sides of Mount E. Dr. Dieffenbach, who first ascended this mountain, in Dec. 1839, found the snow-line at a point about 1,500 ft. below the summit. Vegetation had long ceased before reaching the snow-line, not from the great elevation, however, but from the entire absence of even a patch of soil where plants might take root. The cone of cinders and scoriaceous lava forming the summit, rises from a platform from which it is separated by a deep saddle. The branches or buttresses which Mount E. throws out | the dep. of the Indre, arrond. of La Chatre.-The EGU towards the sea-coast and to the interior, being of inferior height, the cone itself appears to be very isolated. The subjoined outline is from Dr. Dieffenbach's work, a representing Waimatipah, and b Mount E. as seen from near a.
EGMONT (Port), a harbour on the N coast of West Falkland island, in S lat. 51° 21', W long. 60° 15'. The rise and fall of tide here is about 9 ft.
EGNACH, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgau, 21 m. NW of Arbon. Pop.
EGOAS, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas- Geraes, flowing into the Paracatu on the l. bank.— Also a river in the prov. of Bahia, descending from the Serra-Tabatinga, and flowing into the Cor-
EGORAPAN, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Canara, 16 m. S of Carwar.

EGREMONT, a town and parish of England, in Cumberland, 5 m. SSE of Whitehaven, situated 2 m. E of St. George's channel, on the western bank of the Ehen. Area 2,850 acres. Pop. 1,750. E. is one of the polling-places for the members for the E division of the county. It gives the title of Earl to the Wyndham family.—Also a p. of Carmarthenshire, 4 m. NW of Narberth, situated on the East Cleddau. Pop. 140.
EGREMONT, a township in Berkshire co., in the state of Massachusetts, intersected by the Housatonic river. Pop. 1,038.
EGREVE (SAINT), a small town of France, in the dep. of Isère, 6 m. NNW of Grenoble. Pop. 1,260.
EGREVILLE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Lorrez-le- Bocage. Pop. 1,477.
EGRIBUDJAK, a town of Turkey, in the sanjak of Monastir, on the l. bank of the Inji-Karasu, 35 m. SW of Salonica.
EGRIPO. See NEGROPONT.
EGRYAR, a town of Great Bukharia, 18 m. S of Samarcand.

EGTON, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. WSW of Whitby. Area 13,570 acres. Pop. 1,128. —Also a chapelry in the p. of Ulverstone, in Lancashire, 4 m. NNE of Ulverstone, and W of the river Leven. Pop. 547.
EGUILLES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, 6 m. NW of Aix. Pop. 1,587.
EGUISHEIM, or Exen, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Soultz, near the l. bank of the Lauch, 4 m. SW of Colmar. Pop. 2,117.
EGUSU-DAGH, a mountain-range in Turkey, separating Servia and Macedonia, and connected on the W with the Schar-dagh, on the E with the Dupindscha.
EGUZON, a canton and commune of France, in com. is on the l. bank of the Creuse, 20 m. SW of La Chatre.
EGWAID, a river of Wurtemberg, which runs into the Danube between Hochstett and Dillingen.
EGYEEL, a town of Hungary, in the com. and 40 m. ESE of Odenburg. Pop. 600.

EGYPT. An interesting and important region of Africa. It has been called "the connecting link between Africa and the civilized world;" and it belongs at once to classic and to sacred geography, as well as to the modern political geography of the present day. Unique in its antiquities, and remarkable in its physical features,-venerable in its history, and politically and commercially important in its position and resources,-'the land of Egypt' offers to the eye of the man of science and letters, the statesman, and the philanthropist, one of the most interesting portions of the Eastern world. "Philologists, astronomers, chemists, painters, architects, and physicians, must return to E. to learn the origin of writing,—a knowledge of the calendar and solar motion,-the art of cutting granite with a copper chisel, and of giving elasticity to a copper sword,—of making glass of the variegated hues of the rainbow,-of moving single blocks of polished syenite 900 tons in weight, for any distance by land or water, -of building arches round and pointed, with masonic precision unsurpassed at the present day, and antecedent by 2,000 years to the Cloaca Magna of Rome,—of sculpturing a Doric column 1,000 years before the Dorians are known in history,—of fresco painting in imperishable colours,-and of practical knowledge in masonry. Every craftsman can behold in Egyptian monuments the progress of his art 4,000 years ago; and whether it be a wheelwright building a chariot,—a shoemaker drawing his twine,—a leather-cutter using that selfsame form of knife which is considered the best form now,—a weaver throwing the same hand-shuttle,—a whitesmith using that identical form of blowpipe, but lately recognised to be the most efficient,—the seal-engraver cutting in hieroglyphics such names as Shoopoo's 4,300 years ago,—or even the poulterer removing the pip from geese,-all these, and numerous other astounding evidences of Egyptian priority in every art and scheme, usage and custom of civilized life, now require but a glance at the plates of Wilkinson and Rosellini." [Freemason's Quarterly Review.] The interest arising from these and many other sources has derived an extraordinary increase of intensity from recent events. The discoveries of Burckhardt and Banks, Drovetti and Caillaud, Buckingham and Belzoni, Waddington and Hanbury, of Hamilton, Leake, Vyse, and Wilkinson, have made the present generation familiarly acquainted with the caverns and temples and pyramids of E.,—her gods and mummies and amulets, -after having been invisible to Europeans for nearly a score of centuries; Rosellini, Young, and Champollion have lifted the veil from her mysterious hieroglyphics, and unrolled those historical records which baffled the scrutiny of Grecian sages; and, as if to respond to this growing enthusiasm on the part of Europe, a political chief appeared in the person of the late pasha of E., whose policy-though not always sound in principle-has done more for the regeneration of that country, within the last 30 years, than all his predecessors effected during as many centuries. Boundaries. This country is bounded on the N by the Mediterranean; on the E by an imaginary line drawn from Khan-Jounes, at the SW extremity of Syria, to the N extremity of the Arabian gulf or PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL PAPEL cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1841, 7,752.—The | Red sea, which divides it from Asia, and forms the chief part of the E boundary; on the S by Nubia, the frontier being here determined by an imaginary line drawn from the bottom of the gulf of Immonde, on the parallel of 24° 23' and meridian of Khargeh; and on the W by the deserts of Libya and Barca, so as to include the great and Little Oases, the Oasis of Dakhel, and that of Farafre. The N coast is low and sandy, and offers no remarkable point except Cape Burlos; that along the Arabian gulf is more extensive, rocky, and abrupt, and towards the S is bordered with small islands. Extent.] According to D'Anville's map, this country is situated between 23° 40' and 31° 28' N lat., and 30° and 34° 36' E long., which makes its length from N to S 468 geog. m. or about 530 British m., and its greatest breadth, from E to W, 230 geog. m. or 264 British m.; but its breadth in general is a great deal less. Modern French geographers place Cape Burlos, the extreme N point of E., in 31° 37', and the gulf of Immonde, the extreme S point, in 23° 23' N lat.; but other authors assign different admeasurements both to the length and the breadth of E. The truth is, it is impossible to fix the W boundaries of this country with exactness. According to Agaba they run to "the large and extensive height towards the West," 82 hours from Abousir. Della Cella extends E. still farther, to the neighbourhood of the gulf of Bomba, where Cape Trabuco is reckoned by some geographers as belonging to Tripoli, and by others assigned to E. Some again terminate the S boundary-line at Assuan, and others extend it to Teffa. So little certainty of admeasurement is there in the geography of a country where the truths of geometry, and consequently the fundamental principles of geographical science, are supposed to have been discovered, and which gave birth to Ptolemy, one of the most celebrated of ancient geographers! Templeman reckons the superficial extent of E. at 197,842 English sq. m.; Gatterer and Stein assign to it 140,760 sq. m. Some French geographers estimate it at 20,000 sq. leagues; others at 24,000. The Weimar Almanach for 1848 estimates the area of that portion of the valley of the Nile which belongs to E. at 760 German or 16,140 English sq. m.; while the area of the desert sands on each side of the great valley, but politically included within the boundaries of E., amounts to 9,800 German, or 208,132 English sq. m. These, and all other similar calculations, must of course be mere approximations, founded on rather vague data; and it ought to be recollected that this extent of surface is merely nominal in point of value; for, with the exception of the Delta, and the narrow valley of the Nile, or about one-tenth of the whole, the rest of the country is a mere desert. Divisions. E. has been politically divided since a very early period into three parts, which are distinctly marked out by their physical features also, namely: Modern Provinces. Vills. Pop. in 1821. I. UPPER EGYPT. Called by the ancients the 1. Thebes and Esneh, 195 86,888 Thebaid, and by the Arabs Said; and extending from 2. Girjeh or Jirjeh, 374 326,160 Syene to Chemnis.—Area of the Nile valley 120 Ger- 3. Siut, 306 189,900 man sq. m.

EHINGEN, a bail. and town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, 15 m. SW of Ulm, on the l. bank of the Danube. Pop. 2,679. It consists of two divisions, distinguished as the high and low town; and contains several handsome edifices, amongst which are the town-house, and the churches of Sainte Marie and Saint Blaise. It has also a well endowed hospital, and a Catholic gymnasium; and possesses a cotton spinning-mill, a dye-work, and several woollen manufactories. The bail. contains 27,160 inhabitants.
EHLE, a small river of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, which flows into the Elbe, from the E, 3 m. SE of Magdeburg.
EHNHEIM (NIEDER), or NIEDERNAY, a town of France, in the dep. of the Bas-Rhin, cant. of Ober- Ehnheim, 15 m. N of Schelestatt, and about the same distance SW of Strasburg, on the Ergers. Pop. 1,210.
EHNHEIM (OBER). See OBERNAI.

EHNINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, bail. and 4 m. SW of Böblingen, on the r. bank of the Würm. Pop. 1,431.—Also a town in the circle of the Schwarzwald, bail. and 6 m. E of Urach, on the Achalm. Pop. 4,908. It has some linen and lace manufactories. Е-НО. See Y-но.

EHRANG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle and 5 m. N of Trèves, on a rising ground near the confluence of the Kyll with the Moselle. Pop. 950. It has extensive blast-furnaces, and manufactories of hardware.

EHRENBERG, a fort and pass of Tyrol, in the upper circle of the Innthal, 16 m. N of Imst, and 7 m. S of Fussen, near the r. bank of the Lech. EHRENBERG (OBER and UNTER, or ALT and NEU), two contiguous villages of Bohemia, in the circle and 36 m. NNE of Leitmeritz. Pop. 3,899, of whom 2,579 belong to Alt E. Linen and cotton fabrics, and articles in wood are manufactured here.

EHRENBREITSTEIN, a small town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle of Coblenz, on the r. bank of the Rhine, opposite Coblenz, with which it is connected by a bridge of boats 485 ft. in length, and near the confluence of the Moselle. Pop. 2,100. It is well-built, and has two Catholic churches. Tobacco and glue form its chief articles of manufacture. At one extremity of it is a small spring which yields a slightly acidulous effervescent water. In the immediate vicinity of the town is a rocky eminence 468 ft. in height, and surmounted by the often battered fort of E. This fortress, originally a Roman castrum, was destroyed by the French in 1801. It has since been restored by the Prussian government, is capable of containing a garrison of 14,000 men, and is abundantly supplied with water from wells 300 feet in depth. A subterraneous communication cut through the solid rock is said to connect it with Coblenz.
EHRENBURG, a bail, and town of Hanover, in the co. and 21 m. WSW of Hoya, and 18 m. NE of Diepholz. Pop. 66.

EHRENFRIEDERSDORF, or IRBERSDORF, a town of Saxony, in the circle of Zwickau, 4 m. W of Wolkenstein, and 46 m. SW of Dresden, on the slope of the Sauberg and Rohrgraben, at an alt. of 1,735 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 2,150. It has manufactories of parchment, brocade, hosiery, and lace; and extensive mines of tin, silver, iron, and arsenic, were
EHRENHAIN, a village of Saxe-Altenburg, SE of Altenburg. Pop. 507.

EHRENHAUSEN, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Styria, circle and 13 m. N of Marburg, on the r. bank of the Mur, which is here crossed by a bridge, and on the Vienna and Trieste railway. Pop. 500. It is commanded by an ancient fortress.

EHRENSBRUNN, or Ernstbrunn, a town in the archd. of Austria, in the lower circle of Manhartsberg, 14 m. N of Korneuburg. Pop. 4,380. In the environs is a quarry of fine marble; to the NW is a forest of the same name.
EHRENSTEIN, a bail. and village in the principality of Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt, which takes its name from an ancient castle, situated 8 m. WNW of Rudolstadt.
EHRENSTETTEN, a market town of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, bail. and 2 m. NNE of Staufen, and 6 m. SW of Freiburg, on the Mohlinsbach. Pop. 1,564.
EHRESHOFEN, or Ereshoven, a village of Prussia, in the regency and 23 m. ENE of Cologne, and 9 m. SSE of Wipperfurth. It has a magnificent castle; and possesses several iron-works.
EHRICH, a town of Schwarzburg-Sondershausen, bail. of Klingen, 12 m. S of Sondershausen, on the Benne, an affluent of the Helbe. Pop. 1,000. Thread is the chief article of local manufacture.

EIBACH, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, bail. and 2 m. NE of Geislingen, on a stream of the same name, an affluent of the Fils. Pop. 580. It contains a castle, and has manufactories of tobacco.
EIBAU (ALT and NEU), two contiguous villages of Saxony, in the circle of Budissen, 5 m. from Herrnhutt, and 11 m. NW of Zittau, on the Rumburger. Pop. 4,900. It is noted for its linen manufactures.

EIBELSTADT, a small town of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Franconia, on the r. bank of the Main, 6 m. SE of Wurzburg. Pop. 2,000. It is noted for its wine. EIBENSCHITZ, EWANCICE, or WANCICE, a town of Moravia, in the circle of Znaim, on the l. bank of the Iglawa, at the confluence of the Ostawa, 12 m. WSW of Brünn. Pop. 3,549, of whom in 1834, 877 were Jews. The environs are noted for their abundant leguminous crops; and excellent potter's clay is obtained here.

EIBENSTOCK, a bailiwick and town of Saxony, in the circle and 16 m. SSE of Zwickau, near the r. bank of the Schwarzwasser, an affluent of the Mulde, at an alt. of 2,060 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 4,666. It is well built, and has a custom-house. Articles in white-iron, chemical substances, tobacco, brandy, muslin, and lace, are manufactured here. In the environs are iron mines.
EIBERGEN, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Gelderland, 22 m. E of Zutphen, on the l. bank of the Borkul. Pop. 4,265.
EIBESWALD, a town of Styria, in the circle and 26 m. WNW of Mahrburg, in a valley watered by the Sulm. It has a castle, and possesses several iron and glass-works. Coal is wrought in the environs.

EICH, a commune and village of Belgium, in the duchy and 11 m. N of Luxemburg. Pop. 4,591. It has several potteries, paper and wire mills, and blast-furnaces.—Also a parish and v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Lucerne, bail. of Sur See, on the NE side of the lake of Sempach. Pop. 527.—Also a v. of Hesse-Darmstadt, 6 m. from Osthofen, near the 1. bank of the Rhine. Pop. 1,342, of whom 283 are Catholics.—Also a hamlet of Bavaria, in the circle of Schwaben, 13 m. S of Kempton, noted for its mineral baths. 178 EID
EICHBERG, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 9 m. SE of St. Gall, and 2 m. SSW of Alstetten. Pop. 600. It has mineral springs.
EICHEL, a river of France, in the dep. of the Bas-Rhin, which flows into the Sarre on the r. bank.

EICHEN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Upper-Rhine, district and 11 m. SE of Schopfheim, and 26 m. S of Freiburg, on a lake of the same name, which, like Lake Zirknitz, periodically disappears. Pop. 400.
EICHENDORF, or Euchendorf, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Bavaria, 7 m. E of Landau, on the r. bank of the Vils. Pop. 784.
EICHENZELL, a village of Kur-Hessen, in the circle and 11 m. SSE of Fulda. Pop. 540. It has a fine country-seat belonging to the elector.
EICHGRABEN, or EICHGRUBE, a village of Saxony, in the circle of Upper Lusatia, 3 m. SSW of Zittau. It has several manufactories of thread, and bleacheries. Pop. 1,800.
EICHHOLZ, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Königsberg, 20 m. SE of Braunsberg. Pop. 248.
EICHHOLZHEIM, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, bail. and NE of Mosbach. Pop. 822.

EICHHORN, or WEWERY, a village of Moravia, in the circle and 10 m. W of Brünn, on the r. bank of the Schwarzawa. Pop. in 1834, 55. It has an extensive powder-mill, and several iron works. On a mountain commanding the v. are the ruins of an ancient fortress. The environs contain a productive mine of lead. EICHSTADT, EICHSTATT, or AICHSTADT, a principality and town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Pfalz. The principality comprises an area of 117 sq. m.; and is subdivided into 4 presidials. Pop. 45,256. Its highest summits are the Arz and the Kesselberg; and the principal streams by which it is watered are the Altmuhl, the Sulz, and the Schwarzach. It produces grain in great abundance, legumes of all kinds, fruit, hops, lint, and wood, and abounds in game and fish. The mountains furnish iron, marble, slate, and freestone. The manufacture of common woollen stuffs, glass-making, and the working of iron form the chief objects of local industry. The trade consists exclusively in the productions of the soil.—This district fell into the hands of the king of Bayaria in 1805, at the peace of Presburg; and was erected by him in 1815 into a principality in favour of Prince Eugene Beauharnais. —The town, which is the capital of the principality, is situated in a fine valley, on the Altmuhl, 42 m. WSW of Regensburg, and 56 m. NNW of Munich. Pop. in 1846, 7,396. It has 4 suburbs, 3 public squares, a fine castle belonging to the family of Leuchtenberg, a cathedral in which is the tomb of Willibad, several churches, 2 hospitals, an orphan's asylum, a theological and a classical seminary, a gymnasium, and a public library. The manufacture of cloth, linen, pottery, and ironmongery, and breweries form the principal branches of local industry. In the environs are the castles of Wilibaldsburg and Pfünz.
EICHSTETTEN, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, bail, and 6 m. SW of Emmendingen, on the l. bank of the Dreisam. Pop. 2,517, of whom 302 are Jews. Wine is cultivated in the environs.
EICKLINGEN, a bailiwick and village of Hanover, in the gov. of Luneburg, 7 m. SE of Celle. Pop. 250. The bail. contains 4,818 inhabitants.

EIDER, a river of Denmark, which issues from a lake in Holstein, in the bail. of Bordelsholm, 9 m. S of Kiel; runs N and then W, passing through the Westen-see; and 8 m. W of Kiel joins the Kieler or Holstein canal, and forms with it the line of separation between the duchies of Sleswig and Holstein,the former extending E to the fiord of Kiel; the latter flowing W, with considerable sinuosities, to the German ocean, passing in its course Rendsburg, Fredrikstadt, and Tonningen. It runs a total distance of 85 m.; and the action of the tide is felt as far up as Rendsburg. Its principal affluents are the Sorge and the Treen, by both of which it is joined on the r. bank.
EIDERSTED, a village of Denmark, in Jutland, 18 m. SW of Hiörring, near the shore of the Skager- Rack.
EIDERSTEDT, a village of Denmark, in Holstein, SW of Brügge.
EIDFIORD, a village of Norway, on the banks of a lake about 13 m. long, and near the head of the Eidfiord, a branch of the Hardanger-fiord.
EIDFOS, a village of Norway, in the bail. of Jarlsberg, 13 m. from Holmestrand, and 66 m. SW of Christiana. It has extensive iron-works.

EIDGHIR, a small district and town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Hydrabad. It is 54 m. in length, and 21 m. in breadth; and is separated from the district of Bijapur, on the S and W, by the Kristna and the Bima. Its principal towns are Eidghir, Ferozeghur, and Dowletabad. E. is 100 m. WSW of Hydrabad, and about 5 m. from the E bank of the Bima.
EIDINGHAUSEN, a village of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the circle and 7 m. SW of Minden. Pop., inclusive of that of the v. of Wirste, 1,250.
EIDLITZ, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 13 m. NNW of Saatz. Pop. 1,000, chiefly Jews. It has a manufactory of cloth.

EIDSVOLD, a town of Norway, in the prov. of Aggerhuus, 36 m. NNE of Christiana, on the r. bank of the Vermen-Elf. It has some iron forges. This town is noted for the meeting of the national assembly which took place here after the treaty concluded at Kiel on the 14th of January 1814, by which Norway was ceded by Denmark to Sweden.

EIERLAND, a name given to the N part of the island of Texel, in the prov. of N. Holland. It is derived from the number of sea-mews' eggs which are found on its shore. The passage which runs between this part of Texel and the island of Vlieland is named EIERLANDSGAT. EIFEL, EYFFEL, or EIFELGEBIRGE, a mountain range in Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine. It is a ramification of the E chain of the Ardennes—from which it branches near the source of the Roer, and forms a prolongation of one of the SE spurs of the Hohe-Veen. It runs E about 60 m., and terminates on the l. bank of the Rhine near Andernach. Its highest summit, the Snow Eifel, in the circle of Prüm, has an alt. of 2,100 ft. It is remarkable for the wildness of its aspect and the thick forests with which it is covered. The irregular elevated surface is covered with bog, and the thin coating of soil does not afford nourishment for the roots of trees. Every valley, however, is inhabited. Geologists regard it as presenting a series of extinct volcanoes. The principal rivers to which it gives rise are the Nette and Ahr, affluents of the Rhine, and the Kill. Lieser, Alp, and Elz, which flow into the Meuse. Towards its E extremity it encloses the lake named Laagersee, or the Laach lake; and circular maares or meres, to the number of 27, exist within the district.
EIGER, a mountain of Switzerland, in the cant. and 39 m. SE of Berne, and 2 m. N of Monch. It forms one of the principal spurs of the Bernese Alps, and terminates at Grindelwald. Alt. 13,044 ft.

EIGG, or Egg, one of the Western Isles, in the group which forms the p. of Small Isles, in the co. EIM of Inverness, 8 m. W of Arisaig. Area 5,580 Scotch acres. Pop. 546. Its highest summit, Scuir-Eigg, rises to the height of 1,340 ft., and is entirely columnar in its structure. Near the S coast of E. is an islet named Eilan-Chastel.
EIKEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Aargau, 5 m. WSW of Lauffenburg, and 3 m. NW of Frik. Pop. 1,078.
EIL (LOCH), a branch of Loch-Linnhe, which runs NNE from Corran ferry, between the counties of Argyle and Inverness, to Fort William, and thence bends WNW, extending 32 m.

EILAU, or EYLAU (DEUTSCH) or ILAWA, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of W. Prussia, regency and 30 m. ESE of Marienwerder, circle and 13 m. SE of Rosenberg, at the confluence of the Eylanz or Mühlenfluss with lake Geserich. Pop. 2,280. It has tanneries and manufactories of cloth and hats.

EILAU, or EYLAU (PREUSSISCH), a circle and town of Prussia, in the prov. of E. Prussia, regency and 26 m. SSE of Königsberg, near the source of the Pasmar, an affluent of the Frisching. Pop. 2,580. It has a suburb and an old castle; and possesses manufactories of cloth, hats, and leather. It is noted for a victory gained here by the French over the united forces of the Prussians and Russians, on the 8th of February, 1807. Pop. of circle, 30,000.
EILDEN, a village of Norway, in the bail. of N. Drontheim, 65 m. NE of Drontheim, on an affluent of the Oy.

EILDON HILLS, a mountain group in Roxburghshire, in the p. of Melrose, consisting of three conical summits, the central and highest of which is variously estimated at 1,330 and 1,364 ft. above sealevel.

EILENBURG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, regency and 30 m. ENE of Merseburg, circle and 15 m. ESE of Delitzsch, on an island formed by the Mulde, and which is connected by two bridges with the mainland. Pop. in 1837, 7,699; in 1846, 8,740. It is surrounded by ditches, and has an old castle, 2 churches, and an hospital. It has manufactories of vinegar, wax-bleachery, and a dye-work. Hops are cultivated in the environs.
EILENDORF, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, circle and 4 m. E of Aix-la- Chapelle. Pop. 1,146.

EILETHYA, or EL KAB, a village of Upper Egypt, 5 hours above Edfu. The ancient town of E. is a mass of ruins situated upon a bluff close to the river, and surrounded by walls in remarkably good preservation, built of sun-dried bricks 16 inches long, by 6 in. wide, and 4 in. thick. About 1 m. from the ruined town, on the W side of a mountain, are a series of remarkable chambers excavated in the rock, with deep pits for mummies sunk perpendicularly into the floor. These walls are covered with sculpture and paintings.

EILGUN, or ILGUN, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Caramania, sanj. of Akshehr, 28 m. NW of Ladik. It is identified by D'Anville with the ancient Philomelium. It is a thriving town, and well supplied with fruits and provisions.
EILGUNDEL, a district and town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 80 m. NNE of Hydrabad, near the r. bank of the Punnair.
EILSIN, a village of Schaumburg-Lippe, in the bail. and 2 m. ESE of Bückeburg, on the Au, at the foot of the Haarlberg. It is noted for its baths.

EIMBECK, or EINBECK, a town of Hanover, in the gov. of Hildesheim, the cap. of the principality of Grubenhagen, 21 m. NNW of Göttingen, and 40 m. SSE of Hanover, near the l. bank of the Ilm. Pop. in 1846, 5,867. It is enclosed by walls now considerably dilapidated, and has a handsome public square, 3 churches, in one of which are the mausoleums of the dukes of Grubenhagen, an orphan's asylum, 2 hospitals, a gymnasium, and several elementary and industrial schools. The streets are generally narrow, crooked, and ill-paved; but the modern part of the town, erected since the fire in 1826, is well and regularly built. The houses are chiefly of Gothic architecture. It possesses considerable manufactories of linen, stuffs, flannel, hosiery, and tobacco, and several tanneries and bleachfields. The culture of lint, and the rearing of sheep and cattle, form also important branches of local industry. Iron, linen, and wool form its chief articles of trade. In the vicinity is the castle of Rothenkirchen.
EIME, a town of Hanover, in the gov. and 23 m. S of Hanover, near the Leine. Pop. 576.

EIMEO, or MUREA, an island of the South Pacific, in the group of the Society islands, 13 m. WNW of Tahiti. It is 9 m. in length, and about 5 m. in breadth, and is supposed to contain 1,300 inhabitants. Its N end is in S lat. 17° 27' 30"; its centre in W long. 149° 56'. [Biddlecombe.] A ridge of woody mountains crosses the central part of the island, giving it a peculiarly rugged appearance. The valleys are extremely fertile; and in the romantic and beautiful character of its scenery, this island surpasses every other in the Georgian or Society groups. [Ellis.] The coast is steep and rocky, but possesses several good harbours. The best, named Talu or Oponohu, is on the N side. On the NE side is a beautiful lake and village called Tamai. There are a missionary station here, and several trading factories.
EIN. See AIN.

EINDHOVEN, or EYNDHOVEN, a canton and market-town of Holland, in the prov. of N. Brabant, on the l. bank of the Dommel, at the confluence of the Gender, 21 m. SSE of Bois-le-Duc. Pop. 3,028. It is well built; and has two churches and a college. Linen, woollen, and cotton fabrics, and leather, are manufactured here, and form considerable branches of trade.

EINEH, a village of Turkish Armenia, in the pash. of Erzerum, sanj. of Sheherán, to the S of the Giaour-dagh. Snow lies 6 months in the year here; and for more effectual defence from the cold, the houses are sunk under ground.
EINERSHEIM, a seignory and town of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, 6 m. W of Kitzengen. Pop. 740.
EINFISCHTHAL. See Anniviers (Val d').
EINHAUSEN, a village of Saxe-Meiningen, on the Werra, SE of Meiningen. Pop. 300.
EINIEH, a town of Asia Minor, in Anatolia, in the pash, and 25 m. SW of Biga, on the l. bank of the Bokku or Sataldere.
EINOD, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Styria, gov. of Gratz, circle and 51 m. NW of Judenburg. It has an iron-work and some mineral springs.

EINOD, or Soleska, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Carniola, gov. of Laibach, circle and 8 m. WSW of Neustadt, near the l. bank of the Gurk. It has a fine seignorial castle, and contains the ruins of the castle of Alt-Einod.
EINODERBAD, a village of Styria, in the circle and 19 m. WSW of Judenburg, and S of Neumarkt, on the confines of Illyria. It has mineral baths.
EINSIEDEL, a hamlet of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Schwarzwald, bail. of Tubingen, near the Neckar. In the vicinity is an extensive royal domain and agricultural establishment.

EINSIEDEL, or REMETE, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Zips, 18 m. SSE of Leutschau, on the Gölnitz. Pop. 2,370. Iron and copper are wrought in the environs. $ EIS 180 EINSIEDEL, EINSIEDL, HEREMITO-MNECHOW, or Mieschow, a municipal town of Bohemia, in the circle and 38 m. NW of Pilsen, and 75 m. W of Prague. Pop. 806.

EINSIEDELN, or Emsiedeln, a district and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 10 m. NNE of Schwytz, in a valley or large amphitheatre of the same name, and on the r. bank of the Alps. Pop. 3,000. It is celebrated for its abbey, named Notre Dame des Ermites, or Notre Dame d'Einsiedeln, founded about the middle of the 10th cent. on the site of the hermitage of S. Meinrad, and erected into an imperial abbey in 1274. It is of the Benedictine order, and its superior ranks second in dignity at the council of Bâle. It is a vast pile of buildings, situated near the v. in a conspicuous site, at an alt. of 2,985 ft. above sea-level; and comprises a handsome church, a museum, a library of 26,000 volumes, an hospital, and spacious apartments. The church contains an image of the Virgin, noted for its miraculous powers, and annually attracting on the feast-day (14th September) the average number of 130,000 pilgrims, chiefly from the Catholic cantons of Switzerland. In 1828 they amounted to 176,000. The v. consists of about 100 houses, all or nearly all of which are taverns for the accommodation of pilgrims. Inglis has given an interesting account of E., and its pilgrims, in his Tour in Switzerland. The gymnasium, the ecclesiastical school, theological seminary, and savings' bank are the only other institutions worthy of note in this town. Its chief branches of industry are the manufacture of cloth and of other woollen fabrics, dye-works, and the printing and sale of religious books. The reformer Zwingle was curate of this town in 1517.

EINURU, a small town of Hindostan, in the S part of the prov. of Canara, 26 m. NE of Mangalore, and at the foot of the Eastern Ghauts. It contains 9 Hindu temples, numerous pagodas, and a colossal statue of one of their divinities, cut out of a single block of granite. The environs are sterile, and the inhabitants extremely poor.
EINVICK BAY, an indentation of the W coast of Russia, in the gov. of Esthonia, in N lat. 58° 45'.

EINVILLE, or EINVILLE-AU-JARD, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Meurthe, cant. and 5 m. N of Luneville, on the r. bank of the Sanon. Pop. 1,131. It has a fine castle, in which Stanislaus, king of Poland, resided.
EIPELDAU, or LEOPOLDSAU, a village of Austria, in the circle of Mannhartsberg, SE of Korn- Neuburg.
EIRAS, or Heiras, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 5 m. NNE of Coimbra. It has some woollen manufactures.
EIRKE, a parish, partly in Queen's co., and partly in co. Kilkenny, 4 m. NNW of Urlingford. Area 18,584 acres. Pop. 5,678.

EISACH, or EISAK, a river of Tyrol, which takes its rise on the S side of Mount-Brenner, runs SE past Sterzing to Brixen, thence bends SSW, passes Kiausen and Botzen, and unites with the Adige a little below the latter town, after a rapid course of 53 m. Its principal affluent is the Rienz, which it receives on the l. at Brixen.
